    ↓

    Interdisciplinary Scientist

    Multisystem Approach

    My research lies in the intersection between Neuroscience, Cognitivie Science, and Human Physiology.

    Projects

    Exploring the Heart-Brain Connection

    How Our Heart Influences Our Mind

    As a Ph.D. student in Dr. Bradley Voytek's lab at UC San Diego, I am dedicated to uncovering the intricate relationship between heart function and brain activity. My research focuses on how the heart's signals—particularly the shape of the heartbeat wave and the rhythmic patterns in heartbeats—influence neural dynamics, which are crucial for cognitive processes like memory and attention.

    My goal is to understand how age-related changes in heart signals impact working memory, providing valuable insights into the role of heart health in cognitive function. This research highlights the profound connection between our cardiovascular system and brain performance, offering new perspectives on aging and cognitive health.

    About Me

    I am a Neuroscience Ph.D. student at UC San Diego in Dr. Bradley Voytek's Lab. My work specializes in:

    • Advanced computational methods to study heart-brain dynamics in cognitive aging.
    • Understanding nervous system dynamics and their relation to brain health and disease risk across the lifespan.

    Key achievements include:

    • B.S. in Neuroscience from UC Santa Barbara.
    • Recipient of the Morgan Award for Research Promise and a University Award of Distinction.

    Currently, I am advancing my computational expertise to develop innovative tools for neuroscience and cardiovascular research, aiming to address impactful problems and provide practical solutions.

    Download Resume

    Publications

    • Fitzgerald, M. C., Saelzler, U. G., & Panizzon, M. S. (2021). Sex Differences in Migraine: A Twin Study. Frontiers in Pain Research, 2, 113. (paper)
    • Fitzgerald, M. C., Pritschet, L., Santander, T., Grafton, S. T., & Jacobs, E. G. (2020). Cerebellar network organization across the human menstrual cycle. Scientific reports, 10(1), 20732. (paper)

      Posters & Presentations

    • Fitzgerald, M. C. & Panizzon, M. S. American Headache Society. "Mechanisms of Sex Differences in Migraine and the Need to Consider Sex as a Variable of Interest". June 2022. [TALK]
    • Fitzgerald, M. C. & Panizzon, M. S. Organization for the Study of Sex Differences."Hormonal Mechanisms of Sex Differences in Migraine". May 2020. [POSTER]

    Projects

    1. Tool Introduction: Validate our Model and Demonstrate Stability

    This project focuses on introducing and validating a new ECG parameterization tool designed to provide accurate and stable analysis across multiple datasets of healthy individuals. By benchmarking against established datasets like MIT-BIT and PTB-XL, the project aims to demonstrate the stability of waveform shape parameters over time and their relationship with demographic covariates, such as age, sex, and BMI.
    2. Physiological Relevance: Waveform Shape Parameters and Cardiac Pathologies

    This project explores the physiological relevance of waveform shape parameters by applying the ECG tool to a large dataset from the MIMIC IV database. The goal is to identify characteristic ECG features associated with specific cardiac pathologies, such as atrial fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia, and compare these parameters against existing standards, enhancing diagnostic precision.
    3. Time Variance: Probing Variability of Parameters Across Time and Pathologies

    This project investigates the variability of ECG parameters over time within individuals, focusing on how these variations can reveal insights into cardiac pathologies. The research aims to understand the time-varying aspects of ECG parameters, with the potential to detect conditions like arrhythmias based on the consistency of the model's performance over repeated cycles.
